coredata.dc:title: An active equalization strategy for series-connected lithium-ion battery packs based on a dual threshold trigger mechanism
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Hui
Nan, Wenzhi
Liu, Xiaofei
Wang, Fengbin
Chen, Kaiqiang
Chen, Yupeng
coredata.dc:description: It is well acknowledged to all that an active equalization strategy can overcome the inconsistency of lithium-ion cell's voltage and state of charge (SOC) in series-connected lithium-ion battery (LIB) pack in the electric vehicle application. In this regard, a novel dual threshold trigger mechanism based active equalization strategy (DTTM-based AES) is proposed to overcome the inherent inconsistency of cells and to improve the equalization efficiency for a series-connected LIB pack. First, a modified dual-layer inductor equalization circuit is constructed to make it possible for the energy transfer path optimization. Next, based on the designed dual threshold trigger mechanism provoked by battery voltage and SOC, an active equalization strategy is proposed, each single cell's SOC in the battery packs is estimated using the extended Kalman particle filter algorithm. Besides, on the basis of the modified equalization circuit, the improved particle swarm optimization is adopted to optimize the energy transfer path with aiming to reduce the equalization time. Lastly, the simulation and experimental results are provided to validate the proposed DTTM-based AES.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Active equalization strategy
Extend Kalman particle filter
Modified equalization circuit
Improved particle swarm optimization
State of charge difference trigger
Voltage difference trigger

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Single-cell SOC estimation based on EKPF
Energy transfer path optimizer based on IPSO algorithm
Test of the proposed AES based on SOC difference trigger
Test of the proposed AES based on voltage difference trigger

Modified equalization circuit topology with dual-layer inductor.

The DTTM-based AES framework using dual-threshold trigger mechanism.

Flowchart of the proposed EKPF.

The process of the energy transfer path optimizer based on IPSO.

Specific module of the DC2064.

Voltage test results for the modified equalization circuit.
Voltage response. (a)Voltage simulation results. (b) Voltage test results.
The parameters of 2nd-order model.

head.ce:title: An active equalization strategy for series-connected lithium-ion battery packs based on a dual threshold trigger mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Precision Instrument Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is well acknowledged to all that an active equalization strategy can overcome the inconsistency of lithium-ion cell's voltage and state of charge (SOC) in series-connected lithium-ion battery (LIB) pack in the electric vehicle application. In this regard, a novel dual threshold trigger mechanism based active equalization strategy (DTTM-based AES) is proposed to overcome the inherent inconsistency of cells and to improve the equalization efficiency for a series-connected LIB pack. First, a modified dual-layer inductor equalization circuit is constructed to make it possible for the energy transfer path optimization. Next, based on the designed dual threshold trigger mechanism provoked by battery voltage and SOC, an active equalization strategy is proposed, each single cell's SOC in the battery packs is estimated using the extended Kalman particle filter algorithm. Besides, on the basis of the modified equalization circuit, the improved particle swarm optimization is adopted to optimize the energy transfer path with aiming to reduce the equalization time. Lastly, the simulation and experimental results are provided to validate the proposed DTTM-based AES.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to high energy density, long service lifespan, and low self-discharge rate, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been extensively utilized in electric vehicles (EVs) [1]. To meet the driving voltage requirements of EVs, a few of single LIBs are usually arranged in series configurations to establish a battery pack to provide sufficient power. However, there often exists an inconsistency among various lithium-ion cells because it is severe to guarantee the consistency among the materials production, assembly, and fabrication process of LIBs [2–5]. Essentially, this inconsistency can be further aggravated in daily operation with the increasing period of battery usage, which further shortens the useable life of LIB packs [6]. Therefore, it is indispensable to propose an appropriate and effective active equalization strategy (AES) to minimize the inconsistency of LIBs, in expectation of extending the overall performance of the battery pack.

A reliable equalization circuit (EC) is the foundation of developing battery AES. Currently, battery ECs can be categorized into passive equalization circuit (PEC) and active equalization circuit (AEC) [7,8]. For the former one, PEC mainly adopts parallel resistors to dissipate the energy of the LIBs with higher energy in the form of heat to realize equalization. Despite the simple structure of this circuit, it has the shortcomings of high heat dissipation and low efficiency [9,10]. For the latter one, AEC is mainly to transfer high-voltage energy of LIBs to the low-voltage LIBs in the pack through capacitance, transformer, and inductance components, which has the advantage of less dissipating energy and higher equalization speed [11,12]. Due to holding such merits, AEC has been a hotspot with the development of battery EC technologies [13–15]. Yet. The original dual-layer inductor EC usually adopts inductance as the energy transfer element to achieve balance [16]. The balance efficiency of this circuit is higher, and the energy only flows in two contiguous cells. Moreover, if the energy deviation in two non-contiguous LIBs is huge in groups, the energy can only be transmitted via the batteries among them, which may prolong the equilibrium time.

Generally, high-efficiency battery equalization strategy (ES) relies on the selection of equilibrium variables [17]. The commonly-used equalization variables are capacity, voltage, and state of charge (SOC) [18]. In the work of Ref. [19], the capacity was taken as the equalization variable to design the ES, but the capacity was not suitable for online equalization. To this end, an ES based on battery voltage was constructed in Ref. [20] because the voltage was easy to measure. However, it was susceptible to the intrinsic parameters, which made the equalization control performance getting deteriorative [21]. To enhance the equalization efficiency, a class of SOC-based ES was presented in Ref. [22], and this strategy can effectively ameliorate the inconsistency of various cells in the LIB pack. Many studies used SOC as the equilibrium variable to design their expected ES since the SOC was an actual reflection indicator of the inconsistency between cells [23,24]. However, the relationship between the open circuit voltage (OCV) and the SOC in LIBs was often ignored. Especially, there exists a positive correlation between OCV and SOC, and the voltage changes are more obvious than the SOC when the SOC is between 0%-20% and 80%–100% [25]. Therefore, it is not a clever way to take SOC as the only equalization variable to establish desired equalization strategy. In response to this obstacle, an equalization strategy was presented in Ref. [26], which adopted voltage and SOC as the equalization variables simultaneously. Nevertheless, accurate SOC estimation is a difficult issue when developing the appropriate ES.

Based on the above analysis of various ES, a hybrid dual threshold trigger mechanism (DTTM)-based AES is presented to realize the equalization for series-connected LIBs. The DTTM-based AES has such merits as: a modified dual-layer inductor EC makes the batteries in the group form an energy loop, which overcomes the difficulties that the energy only flows between neighboring cells. Besides, based on the modified EC, the improved particle swarm optimization (IPSO) is adopted to obtain the optimum energy transfer path, which further abridges the equalization time. Furthermore, the extend Kalman particle filter algorithm (EKPF) is employed to estimate each single cell's SOC, which reduces the particle degradation in particle filter (PF) algorithm and improves the estimation accuracy. The simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed AES has superiority with respect to the equalization speed and equalization efficiency. The contributions of this study are described as follows:
(1)
A modified dual-layer inductor EC with multiple energy transfer paths is proposed to achieve the energy transfer between non-adjacent batteries, and the IPSO algorithm is used to optimize the transfer path to reduce the energy loss;
(2)
A segmental equalization method of voltage and SOC dual threshold trigger mechanism is proposed to improve the equalization efficiency and the EKPF algorithm is developed to improve the SOC estimation accuracy;
(3)
The hardware experimental platform is developed using the DC2064 equalization circuit board to validate the effectiveness of the proposed AES.

The remanent content of this article is as follows. In Section 2, the modified dual-layer inductor EC is introduced. The proposed AES for a series-connected battery pack system is recommended in Section 3. Section 4 displays the simulation and experimental validation. The conclusion is described in Section 5.

In the original dual-layer inductor EC as shown in Fig. 1 , when the energy deviation between two non-adjacent LIBs in the series-connected battery group-I or group-II is large, the energy can only be transmitted through the intermediate cells between the two non-adjacent cells, which may greatly aggrandize the equalization time [27–29]. To resolve this issue, we use two new equalization modules to connect the first LIB with the last LIB in the group-I and group-II of the first layer circuit, and then form a modified dual-layer inductor EC (as depicted in Fig. 2 ). This circuit can achieve the energy transfer between two non-adjacent LIBs in the group-I or the group-II. For instance, if SOC first_LIB ​> ​SOC last_LIB, in the original EC, the energy is delivered via the LIBs between the first cell and the last cell, whereas, the energy can be directly transmitted by the new equalization module in the modified EC.

Based on the above modified dual-layer inductor EC topology, a series battery pack comprising eight LIBs is employed to derive the expected battery AES, and it is divided into two groups: group-I contains six cells and group-II contains two cells. On the one hand, group-II is used to build a more comprehensive topology circuit that can reflect the balance between battery groups. On the other hand, by dividing the battery packs into different sizes (e.g., six cells for group-I and two cells for group-II), the potential impact of different battery numbers on energy management efficiency, equalization performance, and system stability can be more deeply explored.

To expound the work principle of the modified EC, group-I as shown in Fig. 3 is taken as the research object. If SOC Cell-1>SOC Cell-6, the circuit employs two types of balancing simultaneously. The first type involves balancing between Cell-1 and Cell-6, and the equilibrium process is as follows:
(1)
Discharging of Cell-1: M1a is triggered, Cell-1, M1a, L1a, and D1c form a closed discharge circuit to transform the electrical energy of Cell-1 to the magnetic energy in L1a. And the discharge current flows in the same direction as the red arrow;
(2)
Charging of Cell-6: M1d is triggered, the charge circuit is comprised of Cell-1, M1a, L1a, and D1c, which converts the magnetic energy in L1a into electrical energy. And the charge current follows the blue arrow;
(3)
L1a degaussing: when Cell-6 is finished charging, L1a and R1a are formed the degaussing circuit to consume residual energy to avert magnetic saturation.

Another equilibrium is between two adjacent LIBs in group-I. For instance, when SOC Cell-1>SOC Cell-2, M11 is triggered, the excess energy in Cell-1 is delivered to Cell-6 by L11 until the two LIBs reach equalization M11 is turned off. Herein, M1b and M1c are designed to ensure the flexibility of the proposed EC and meet the energy management requirements under different working modes.

In fact, there exists an identified mapping relationship between OCV and SOC of a certain LIB cell. Especially, in a large SOC operation range, a large SOC change is only caused by a small OCV change. To this end, if battery SOC is adopted as the only equilibrium variable [22–24], the design of the equalization strategy may not be appropriate for battery equalization owing to insufficient voltage equalization performance. The dual-threshold policy sets voltage threshold and SOC difference threshold. If the battery voltage exceeds the threshold, or the SOC difference between batteries exceeds the preset threshold, the system determines that the battery pack SOC is inconsistent. After the equalization control is activated, the energy of the high-voltage batteries is transferred to other batteries until the SOC is evenly distributed. Both battery voltage and SOC are selected as the equilibrium variables to build the desirable AES as shown in Fig. 4 , which is called the DTTM-based AES. In this strategy, the SOC is estimated by the EKPF at first. For the LIBs with SOC between 20% and 80%, SOC is served as the equilibrium variable. Conversely, when SOC is absent from 20% to 80%, voltage is adopted as the equilibrium variable. After that, the IPSO algorithm is employed to obtain the optimal energy transfer path, which makes the modified equalization circuit based on the proposed DTTM-base AES abridge the equalization time.

The 2nd-order RC model demonstrated in Fig. 5 is adopted to characterize the performance of a single cell. U OCV is the OCV; R 1, R 2, C 1, and C 2 are the resistances and capacitances, respectively; U 1 and U 2 denote the voltage at R 1 and R 2, respectively; I represent current; U d signifies the terminal voltage.

The SOC can be calculated by Ref. [30]:
(1)
S O C = S O
C 0
+
∫ μ I d t
Q N
where SOC 0 denotes the initial value of SOC, μ expresses the charge–discharge rate, and Q N signifies the nominal capacity.

In terms of the Kirchhoff's law, its state equation and output equation are derived as:
(2)
x ˙
t
=
S
O ˙
C
U ˙
1
U ˙

(3)

t

Herein, we employ the test devices exhibited in Fig. 6 to collect the required battery data, and the chemistry of the test ternary LIB is LiNi x Co y Mn1-x-y O2 (30 A·h/3.7 V). The test board is mainly comprised of a monitor, a thermostat (HYD-TH-80DH) and a battery tester (ITS5300). It should be noted that all experiments are conducted at 25 ​°C. Under the test of Hybrid Pulse Power Characteristic (HPPC) in Fig. 7 (a), the OCV-SOC relation curve is displayed in Fig. 7(c). In this test process, the battery parameters are identified at an interval of 10% SOC, and the results are illustrated in Table 1 .

From the OCV-SOC curve in Fig. 7(c), the OCV variation is relatively flatter when battery SOC is ranged from 20%−80%. However, when the SOC is between 0% and 20%, and 80%–100%, there reveals a relatively steep trend between battery SOC and OCV. If the SOC or voltage is adopted as the only equilibrium variable, the balance control accuracy and efficiency may be restrained [31]. Thus, it is tough to adequately characterize the inconsistency by choosing an individual equilibrium variable. Therefore, both voltage and SOC are herein adopted as equalization variables to improve the equalization efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single-cell SOC estimation based on EKPF
ce:section.ce:para: In the estimation of SOC by Particle Filter (PF) method, the variance of particle weight will aggrandize with iterations, resulting in degradation, which will affect the final estimation result. To improve this degradation phenomenon, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is adopted as suggested density function in this paper to enhance the estimation accuracy, namely EKPF, which combines the linearized ability of EKF and the observed capability of PF algorithm [31,32]. Fig. 8 indicates the workflow of the designed EKPF algorithm. This EKPF algorithm applies EKF in the resampling phase to generate a reasonable suggested density distribution for each particle, so as to acquire more precise estimation. Therefore, we employ EKPF to predict the single cell's SOC.

Based on the working principle of the above EKPF algorithm, the estimation accuracy of this algorithm is simulated and verified under dynamic urban dynamometer driving schedule (UDDS) condition. The results are exhibited in Fig. 9 .

Overall, the SOC results predicted by the PF and EKPF get well accordance with the reference value under the UDDS conditions (as shown in Fig. 9). From the subplot in Fig. 9(b), the red solid-line (EKPF) is particularly close to the black line (Reference). The PF-based SOC estimation error is kept within 5%, while the EKPF-based SOC estimation error is kept within 2%, which further indicates that the proposed EKPF method has a higher SOC estimation accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy transfer path optimizer based on IPSO algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: This section introduces the energy transfer process by taking group-I of six cells in the first layer equalization circuit as an example, see Fig. 2. It is assumed that SOC Cell-1 > SOC Cell-6, as shown in Fig. 10 , there are two energy transfer paths, the one is from Cell-1 to Cell-6, and the other is from Cell-2, Cell-3, Cell-4, and Cell-5 to Cell-6. The summation of energy transfer for the two paths is different with each other, it is better to use an appropriate optimization algorithm to obtain the optimal transfer path. Owing to its advantages of high convergence speed and easy implementation, the PSO algorithm has been widely used in optimization. However, there are issues related to local optimization and pre-mature convergence, which may lead to inaccurate selection of weights. To address this problem, we adopt adaptive weights to reduce the weight coefficient to further improve the ability of executing an overall search in PSO, and the improved PSO (IPSO) algorithm is employed to acquire the optimal intra-group energy transfer path.

The specific working principle of IPSO is as follows. It is assumed that a swarm of N particles is searching for the best position in a D-dimensional, then each particle can be assigned a position as shown in Eq. (4):
(4)
X i
=
x
i 1
,
x
i 2
, ⋯ ,
x
i D
,
i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , N

After the position of particles have been assigned, the speed and location of the particle should be updated. The speed of the i th particle is written as:
(5)
V i
=
v
i 1
,
v
i 2
, ⋯ ,
v
i D
,
i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , N

The optimal position searched by the i th particle is referred to the individual extreme value
P best
:
(6)
p best
=
p
i 1
,
p
i 2
, ⋯ ,
p
i D
,
i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , N

The optimal position found by the entire particle swarm up to the current iteration is known as the global extreme value, which is expressed by:
(7)
g best
=
p
g 1
,
p
g 2
, ⋯ ,
p
g D

When the optimal values of the p best and g best are founded, the particle updates its velocity and position in Eqs. (8) and (9). Therein, we use Eqs. (10) and (11) to dynamically adjust the weight to improve the variety of the particles during the early phase of the algorithm and the convergence rate of the optimal solution in the later phase of the algorithm as:
(8)
v i
= ω ·

r 1

v i
, i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , N
(10)

× tan

where ω max and ω min represent the maximum and minimum number of iterations, respectively; t and t max denote current iteration number and the maximum iteration number, respectively; σ is inertia adjustment coefficient; betarnd represents a random number generator that can generate random numbers in MATLAB; c 1 and c 2 signify the acceleration constants.

To better understand the optimization process, a closed energy loop is formed in the modified EC as shown in Fig. 10. Each circle denotes the gap in the SOC of a single cell and the average SOC of this group.

The average SOC of this battery group is calculated by:
(12)
S O C
¯
n
=
(
S O
C 1
+ S O

+ ⋯ + S O
C n
)
/ n

The deviation between the SOC of single cell in the groups and the mean SOC of this group can be expressed by:
(13)
D i

,
n = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , n
when D
i is positive, the i th cell is discharged, and when D
i is negative, the i th cell is charged.

The energy transmit of the i th LIB can be counted by:
(14)
T i
=
{
T 1
,
i = 1

T
i − 1
,
i = 2 , 3 , ⋯ , n

The summation of energy transduction can be expressed by:
(15)
T sum
=
|
T 1

+
|
T 2
|
+ ⋯ +
|
T n
|

According to Eqs. (14) and (15), we can see that the value of the minimum value of T sum depends on the value of T 1, so the IPSO algorithm is adopted the optimal value of T 1 and acquire the optimal energy transfer path.

In the modified EC, the SOC of the group-I containing the six batteries in the first layer circuit was set as 58.6%, 52.0%, 32.6%, 42.2%, 61.2% and 50.4% respectively, and the minimal summation of SOC transfer is counted by the IPSO. Fig. 11 shows the iterative process.
From Fig. 11, it can be observed that the designed IPSO algorithm has a faster convergence rate compared with the conventional PSO algorithm.

The balancing trigger mechanism of the first layer equalization circuit is formed as:
(16)
S O
C i
− S O
C
ave − m
≥ 0.05 ,
i = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , n ,
m = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , n
(17)
S O
C i
− S O
C
ave − m
≤ 0.01

The equalization of the second layer circuit is controlled by the SOC difference between the two battery packs as shown in Eqs. ​(18) and (19):
(18)
S O
C
pack − 1
− S O
C
pack − 2
≥ 0.05
(19)
S O
C
pack − 1
− S O
C
pack − 2
≤ 0.01

In this section, a battery pack composing of eight series-connected LIBs is employed to verify the DTTM-based AES, and those eight cells are divided into two series-connected battery groups: group-I and group-II. The group-I includes Cell-1 to Cell-6 and the group-II consists of Cell-7 and Cell-8.

Herein, the corresponding simulation model of the battery pack are constructed in MATLAB/Simulink, the parameters of the simulation model: inductance L is 1H, field-discharge resistance R is 10 ​kΩ, the on-resistance of the MOS is 50 ​mΩ, line resistance is ignored, the duty cycle is 50% and the diode is default parameter from Simulink. The simulation model as shown in Fig. 12 .

To verify the superiority of the proposed AES, the simulations of the modified and original dual-layer inductor EC are carried out and contrasted under three different working conditions (static condition, charging and discharging condition, and UDDS condition) in this section. The initial SOC values for the eight LIBs are 70%, 55%, 68%, 50%, 60%, 40%, 80% and 45%, respectively.

In Fig. 13 , it is seemed that obvious SOC deviations between the whole battery string of the two ECs will be reduced to a small value (2%) by provoking the SOC difference trigger under the static condition. The variations of battery SOC in the two circuits further exhibit the availability of the proposed battery AES. Additionally, it can also be observed that the original dual-layer inductor EC without path optimization shown in Fig. 13(a) needs 5649s to reach the final equalization state. Yet, the modified dual-layer inductor EC with the designed AES, see Fig. 13(b), needs 4818s to fulfill the equalization, which means that the equalization time is saved about 14.7%.

We also compared the two ECs equipped with SOC difference trigger in charging and discharging conditions. The SOC variation trajectories of these two ECs under charging and discharging conditions are displayed in Fig. 14 .

Fig. 14(a)–(b) illustrate that the original dual-layer inductor EC needs 5,647 ​s to achieve the equalization, and the modified dual-layer inductor EC takes 4,676 ​s to reach equalization under the charging condition. In contrast, the modified dual-layer inductor EC abridges the equalization time about 971 ​s. Whereas, under the discharging condition, the SOC deviations in the original dual-layer EC takes 5,650 ​s to keep in a certain range in Fig. 14(c). Meanwhile, the modified dual-layer inductor EC only needs 4,937 ​s to realize the equalization, which is displayed in Fig. 14(d). According to the above discussions, we can see that no matter in the charging or discharging condition, the SOC variations of the two circuits can eventually reach unanimity, which reveals the availability of the proposed AES. Besides, the equalization time of the modified EC is less than that of the original EC.
To simulate the SOC changes of an EV in actual operation, the simulation experiments of the two ECs are performed under UDDS current condition, and the SOC variation curves are exhibited in Fig. 15 .

Obviously, the SOC deviations of the two ECs can finally be maintained within a certain range in the UDDS condition. And the original dual-layer inductor EC achieves balance in 5,769 ​s. Conversely, the modified dual-layer inductor EC only needs 4,991 ​s to reach the equalization state, the results are demonstrated in Fig. 15(b). Hence, the modified circuit can upsurge the efficiency, which further proves the superiority performance of our proposed AES.

To further illustrate the validity of the DTTM-based AES, a test platform is constructed displayed in Fig. 16 , which contains a series-connected battery pack, an ITS5300 battery tester with eight sampling channels, a host computer for user–machine interface and data acquisition, and a DC2064 circuit board with a low-cost MCU of S9KEA128A.

The DC2064 is used as the core device for battery voltage monitoring. When an imbalance between batteries is detected, the DC2064 initiates the equalization through the control strategy. Specifically, when a battery voltage is larger than the set threshold, the DC2064 transfers excess energy to lower-voltage cells by controlling the corresponding MOS. Additionally, the developed equalization scheme in MATLAB/Simulink is written into this DC2046 board via USB device, which is shown in Fig. 17 .

In the equalization experiment platform, the battery pack is comprised of eight ternary LIB cells, and the ITS5300 system records the current and voltage data of the battery by the sampling channels. The experimental steps are as follows: First, the initial SOCs of these eight LIBs varied in 20%–80% are selected to demonstrate the availability of our presented AES based on the SOC difference trigger. Next, four LIBs (Cell-5 to Cell-8) in the series-connected battery pack are chosen as the research object to validate the availability of our AES based on the voltage difference trigger, and the initial voltage values of those four cells are 4.075 V, 3.591 V, 3.547 V, and 4.022 V, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of the proposed AES based on SOC difference trigger
ce:section.ce:para: For test validation of the proposed AES based on SOC difference trigger, the series-connected single cells with the initial SOC values (70%, 55%, 68%, 50%, 60%, 40%, 80% and 45%) are adopted to implement the DTTM-based AES, and the experimental results show in Fig. 18 .

It is indicted in Fig. 18(a) that the SOC deviations in the two ECs gradually converge to a small range, and 84.5 ​min is needed to reach the equalization state in the original dual-layer inductor EC without energy path optimization connected LIB pack. Besides, by adding the energy transfer path optimizer in the modified dual-layer inductor EC, the equalizing duration is decreased considerably to 71.1 ​min, see Fig. 18(b), which abridges 13.4 ​min compared with the original dual-layer inductor EC without the energy transfer path optimizer. The SOC variations demonstrate that the proposed AES based on SOC difference trigger has good performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of the proposed AES based on voltage difference trigger
ce:section.ce:para: This part reveals an experimental verification of the proposed AES. The four LIBs (Cell-5 to Cell-8) are used as the research objects in this experiment. Since the voltage equalization is same in the two ECs, we only performed voltage equalization verification in the modified EC. The experimental bench and the corresponding results are displayed in Figs. 19 and 20 .

As Fig. 19 shows, the initial voltages of the four LIBs are 3.547 V, 4.022 V, 3.591 V and 4.075 V, respectively. And the preliminary voltage deviations are greater than 0.05 V, so the voltage difference triggers are provoked to start the equalization process. The final voltage values after equalization are 3.798 V, 3.803 V, 3.797 V and 3.808 V, respectively. Besides, the voltage deviations after equalization are 0.02V. The voltage variation of the four cells proves the effectiveness of the proposed AES based on voltage difference trigger mechanism. Fig. 20 displays the voltage trajectories of both the simulation and experimental results.

Fig. 20(a) introduces the simulation voltage trajectories of these four cells, which converges to the equalization state around 145 ​min. Meanwhile, the experimental voltages of the four cells take about 172 ​min to attain equilibrium. Notably, the equalization time required by the simulation is less than that needed by the experiment because the simulation is carried out in a relatively ideal environment, yet the experimental equalization process needs more time because there might exist some unpredictable resistances and insufficient electrical conductivity in the ECs [33]. Combined with the simulation and experimental results, it can be inferred that the proposed AES based on the voltage trigger is effective.

Battery imbalance would result in the insufficient energy utilization of the entire serially connected battery pack. To overcome this issue, faced in the design and implementation of equalization control strategy, a dual threshold trigger mechanism based active equalization strategy is developed in this paper. In which, a modified dual-layer inductor EC for a series-connected LIB pack is first designed to make it possible for the energy transfer path optimization. Then, a single-cell SOC estimator is developed using the integrated EKPF algorithm, and by adding an equalization module in the first layer circuit of the modified dual-layer inductor EC, the battery energy of the series-connected LIB pack can be transferred directly between non-adjacent batteries, which facilitates the implementation of energy transfer path optimizer based on the IPSO algorithm. Extensive simulation and experimental results reveal the effectiveness the proposed DTTM-based AES under the static condition and the UDDS condition.

Although the DTTM-based AES has good performance in simulation and experiments, it is more complicated than that of the original strategy, which means more computational efforts are required in the modified strategy. The main tasks for reducing the computational cost of the DTTM-based strategy and implementing it in a real car constitute our future work.
The data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Hui Pang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Wenzhi Nan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiaofei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Fengbin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Kaiqiang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Yupeng Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that we have no known competing financial interests, personal relationships, or work that could be perceived to influence the reported results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Artificial intelligence technology project of Xi'an Science and Technology Bureau (No. 21RGZN0014).
